critique.summary: The article explains the concept of a "molly guard," a protective cover or mechanism designed to prevent accidental pressing of important buttons, often named after an engineer's daughter who accidentally pressed a critical button. It discusses examples of molly guards in hardware and software, and introduces the idea of "reverse molly guards," which are buttons that activate automatically after inactivity.
mainPoints: A molly guard is a plastic safety cover or mechanism that prevents accidental pressing of significant buttons.
The term allegedly originates from an engineer's daughter named Molly who accidentally pressed a big red button in a datacenter.
Molly guards appear in various forms in hardware, such as recessed buttons, plastic ridges, and SIM card ejection holes.
In software, molly guards manifest as confirmation dialogs or requiring multiple key presses (e.g., Ctrl+Alt+Del).
Reverse molly guards are buttons that activate themselves after a period of inactivity, which can be helpful in some programming contexts.
critique.truthfulness: The concept of a molly guard as a protective cover for buttons is well-known in engineering and safety design. The anecdote about the engineer's daughter named Molly is a commonly cited but unverified origin story, making it more of a folklore than a confirmed fact. The examples of molly guards in hardware and software are accurate and consistent with known safety mechanisms. The idea of reverse molly guards is less commonly discussed but plausible as a design pattern. Overall, the article's claims are mostly accurate, though the origin story should be treated as anecdotal rather than factual.
considerations: The article does not provide verifiable sources or multiple perspectives to confirm the origin story of the term 'molly guard.'
It does not discuss any potential drawbacks or criticisms of molly guards, such as user inconvenience or failure modes.
The article lacks technical details or references to standards or best practices related to molly guards in engineering.
The concept of reverse molly guards could be expanded with examples or discussion of contexts where they are beneficial or problematic.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the concept of 'Molly Guard' as a safety mechanism to prevent accidental actions, with examples from software and hardware. Commenters share real-world analogies, personal anecdotes, and related concepts like poka-yoke and defensive design, while some debate the applicability of such guards in software.

comments.author: 0xbadcafebee
comments.text:
In DevOps (and Lean, TPS) the more advanced form of this is the Poka-Yoke (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Poka-yoke). Poka-yokes don't just add safety, they also guide the human away from making a mistake.

The canonical example is the automatic shift knob in a car. The shift knob is designed to 1) prevent you from accidentally shifting all the way back into reverse without pressing the shift button, and 2) prevents you from leaving park or neutral without depressing the brake pedal. This way you don't damage the drivetrain or accidentally cause the car to roll forward/backward.

Poka-yoke is a form of defensive design (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Defensive_design). For a beautiful example of defensive design, see the average electric kettle. If water boils over the top it won't short the device, if it boils dry it'll stop operating, the handle and body are plastic to prevent burning yourself, the handle is ergonomic to make carrying 1.5L of sloshing boiling water not cause you to spill it, the cord is detached from the kettle so you don't yank the cord and spill the boiling water, the switches are located on the bottom away from hot steam, and the lids usually lock while in operation, again to prevent damage from spillage or steam. It's the simplest and safest possible way to boil water, and it's $20.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a well-informed explanation of poka-yoke as a form of defensive design, giving relevant examples and linking to authoritative sources, making it highly accurate and insightful.

comments.author: canucker2016
comments.text:
Samsung learned about molly guards the hard way - recall of millions of products after accidental fires from people/pets activating the front-panel dials.

see https://arstechnica.com/tech-policy/2024/08/samsung-recalls-...

  Luckily, there’s an easy solution recently devised that can prevent this safety hazard in homes across America, Samsung said. Customers concerned about unintentional activations can request free knob locks and covers that Samsung confirmed made it much harder to accidentally turn on the stove.

  During the meeting, the CPSC shared data showing that across 338 incidents between January 1, 2018, and May 30, 2024, stoves from “ten specific manufacturers” were involved in fires causing 31 injuries and two deaths. Additionally, the CPSC had recorded “two other fatal incidents where a range was accidentally turned on when a knob was bumped, but the manufacturer is unknown.”

  ...Companies said the CPSC data would help them “fully understand the issues” and “make sure that reasonable and foreseeable circumstances would be addressed” without impacting compliance with the Americans with Disabilities Act.
After mentioning this article to relatives, one said they had nixed buying one product because of the front dials. Then we heard from a relative in another city who bought a house due to a newborn baby - one of the additional purchases was a oven/stove/range with front panel dials.
comments.analysis: This comment cites a recent real-world incident with a credible source, illustrating the importance of molly guards in preventing accidents, thus providing a strong practical example.

comments.author: JoshTriplett
comments.text: There's a great piece of software called "molly-guard", which intercepts calls to "poweroff" and "reboot" and similar. It checks if it's being invoked via an SSH session, and if so, it asks you to type the name of the system you're shutting down. That way, you never accidentally shut down a remote server when you meant to shut down your own system (or a different server).
comments.analysis: This comment accurately describes a known software tool named molly-guard, explaining its function clearly and relevantly to the discussion.

comments.author: RadiozRadioz
comments.text:
> There is no worse feeling for a programmer than waking up, walking up to the machine that was supposed to work through the night, and seeing it did absolutely nothing, stupidly waiting for hours for a response to a question that didn't even matter.

No, there's one worse feeling. Walking up to the machine that was supposed to work throughout the night, and seeing it had a surprise update that rebooted the system.

One of my favorite things about ditching Windows.
comments.analysis: This comment adds a personal perspective on system reliability and unexpected reboots, which is a common and relatable issue, though more anecdotal than factual.

comments.author: wibbily
comments.text:
Fun: the “Molly” in question is Ed Krol’s daughter - he’s the guy who wrote the Whole Internet User’s Guide and Catalog.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ed_Krol
comments.analysis: This comment provides a factual and interesting background detail about the origin of the term 'Molly' in molly-guard, adding context to the discussion.

comments.author: stevage
comments.text: I feel like modern tv remotes are the opposite of this principle. It is often the case that almost every single button will when pushed in some way interrupt the current program, often jumping out to a different menu or changing to a different program or something. It makes handling the remote or trying to change the volume a fraught experience.
comments.analysis: This comment offers a thoughtful critique of user interface design, contrasting it with the molly guard principle, though it is more opinion-based.

comments.author: fainpul
comments.text: Just please don't start adding molly-guards to your software. The concept only makes sense in the physical world, e.g. where the "important button", that you might never have to press, needs to be in reach all the time. In software, there are better solutions.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a reasoned argument about the limitations of molly guards in software, suggesting alternative approaches, which is a valuable perspective.

comments.author: davidshepherd7
comments.text: That page is copied verbatim from https://unsung.aresluna.org/molly-guard-in-reverse/ (which is linked at the top). The original page also has much better formatting.
comments.analysis: This comment points out a source duplication and formatting issue, which is factual but less substantive to the core discussion.

comments.author: clbrmbr
comments.text: I once was a communications contractor for the major NJ power utility. One of their long time field techs (let’s just refer to him as Mr. T) was giving a tour of a substation that was built from the looks of it in the 50s. I have, you see, this bad habit of leaning on things… well Mr. T, without missing a beat, slid his forearm between my hip and a faded green Bakelite knob, the kind that goes in and out rather than twisting. He informed me that if I had leaned any further I would have shut off half of Newark.
comments.analysis: This anecdote illustrates the importance of physical safety measures in critical infrastructure, supporting the molly guard concept with a real-life example.

comments.author: meelford
comments.text: I personally know a guy who shut down an oil factory by pressing the molly guard button, just because the button looked interesting.
comments.analysis: This personal story highlights a failure of a molly guard due to human curiosity, adding nuance but lacking broader evidence.
